statement: [arXiv:0802.3549 Proposition 2.1] Directional filling of absolute cycles in a rectangle. Let R = [0,R_1] × ... × [0,R_n] be a Euclidean rectangle with 0 < R_1 ≤ ... ≤ R_n. For an integral Lipschitz chain C in R^n and a coordinate tuple A of cardinality dim C, write Vol_A(C) for the mass of the orthogonal projection of C to the coordinate plane spanned by the axes in A, counted with geometric multiplicity, equivalently the integral over C of the absolute value of the corresponding coordinate component of the unit tangent multivector. If z is an integral Lipschitz m-cycle in R, with 0 ≤ m < n and ∂z = 0, then there is an integral Lipschitz (m+1)-chain y in R with ∂y = z such that for every coordinate (m+1)-tuple I, if i is the smallest element of I and I∖{i} denotes the m-tuple obtained by deleting i, then Vol_I(y) ≤ R_i Vol_{I∖{i}}(z).
proof_sketch: Induct on n. Let π be orthogonal projection of R to the face x_1 = 0. There is a straight-line homology H from z to π(z), obtained by sliding each point parallel to the x_1-axis. Its only nonzero directional volumes are those involving direction 1: for I = {1}∪J, Vol_I(H) ≤ R_1 Vol_J(z), and if 1 is not in I then Vol_I(H)=0. Since z is a cycle, π(z) is an m-cycle in the (n−1)-rectangle [0,R_2]×...×[0,R_n]. By the induction hypothesis, π(z) bounds an (m+1)-chain y′ in that face with Vol_I(y′) ≤ R_i Vol_{I∖{i}}(π(z)) ≤ R_i Vol_{I∖{i}}(z) for every I not containing 1; for I containing 1, y′ has zero I-volume because it lies in x_1=0. Then y = H + y′ has boundary z and satisfies the asserted estimate. The ordering R_1≤...≤R_n is used only to identify the smallest coordinate in I as the side length appearing in the bound; no loss occurs in the projection because coordinate projections are 1-Lipschitz on directional volumes.

statement: [arXiv:0802.3549 Proposition 2.2] Directional boundary-pushing for relative cycles in a rectangle. Let R = [0,R_1] × ... × [0,R_n] be a Euclidean rectangle with 0 < R_1 ≤ ... ≤ R_n, and define directional volumes Vol_A as in Proposition 2.1. Let z be an integral Lipschitz m-chain in R which is a relative cycle, meaning ∂z is supported in ∂R. Then there exist an integral Lipschitz (m+1)-chain y in R and an integral Lipschitz m-chain B supported in ∂R such that ∂y = z + B, with the following bounds. First, for every coordinate (m+1)-tuple I, let e be the smallest coordinate index not in I, using the harmless convention e=n+1 if I={1,...,n}; then Vol_I(y) ≤ C(n) sum_{d=1}^{e−1} R_d Vol_{I∖{d}}(z). Second, for every coordinate m-tuple J not containing 1, let e be the smallest index in {2,...,n} not in J, again using e=n+1 if J contains all of {2,...,n}; then Vol_J(B) ≤ C(n)[ Vol_J(z) + sum_{d=1}^{e−1} R_1^{-1} R_d Vol_{({1}∪J)∖{d}}(z) ]. Here C(n) depends only on n, and all tuple-deletion expressions in the sums are well-defined by the definition of e.
proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on n, with a special sliding lemma as the basic move. If ∂z avoids the top face x_1=R_1, slide z to the bottom face by f(x,t)=(t x_1,x_2,...,x_n). The swept chain y has ∂y=z+B with B⊂∂R. It has Vol_I(y)≤R_1 Vol_{I∖{1}}(z) when 1∈I and zero I-volume otherwise. For J not containing 1, the only part of B contributing to Vol_J is the bottom projection of z, so Vol_J(B)≤Vol_J(z). For a general relative cycle choose a height h by the coarea inequality so that every directional slice z_h=z∩{x_1=h} satisfies Vol_K(z_h)≤C R_1^{-1}Vol_{{1}∪K}(z). Decompose z as z_1+z_2 with z_2=[0,R_1]×z_h and z_1=z−z_2. Split z_1 into upper and lower pieces; each avoids one of the top or bottom faces, so the sliding lemma applies. The slice choice controls the extra cylinder [0,R_1]×z_h, giving chains y_1,B_1 satisfying the stated estimates. For z_2, apply the induction hypothesis in the (n−1)-rectangle [0,R_2]×...×[0,R_n] to z_h, obtaining y_h with ∂y_h=z_h+B_h and directional estimates. Set y_2=[0,R_1]×y_h. Then ∂y_2=z_2+B_2, where B_2=[0,R_1]×B_h−{0}×y_h+{R_1}×y_h lies in ∂R. If I contains 1, Vol_I(y_2)=R_1 Vol_{I∖{1}}(y_h), and induction plus the slice bounds gives the required sum; if I omits 1, the I-volume is zero. For J omitting 1, the side cylinder [0,R_1]×B_h has zero J-volume, while the two end copies contribute Vol_J(y_h), again controlled by induction and the slice estimate. Adding y=y_1+y_2 and B=B_1+B_2 proves the proposition.

statement: [arXiv:0802.3549 Proposition 3.1] Directional coarea inequality. Let y be a smooth m-chain in R^n, let (N^q,h) be a q-dimensional Riemannian manifold of finite positive volume, and let F:y→N be a smooth map whose q-dilation is at most Λ, i.e. the operator norm of the induced map on q-vectors is everywhere at most Λ. Put k=m−q, assume k≥0, and define directional volumes Vol_A for chains in R^n as in Proposition 2.1. Then there is a fiber z=F^{-1}(p), for some regular value p∈N, such that for every coordinate k-tuple J, Vol_J(z) ≤ C(n) Λ Vol(N,h)^{-1} sum_{I⊃J, |I|=m} Vol_I(y). The sum is over all coordinate m-tuples I containing J, and C(n) depends only on n.
proof_sketch: Apply the general coarea formula with a carefully chosen weight. At a point x where dF has full rank q, the kernel of dF restricted to T_x y is a k-plane; let V(F)(x) be its unit k-vector. For a fixed coordinate k-tuple J, use the weight G(x)=|J·V(F)(x)| in the coarea formula. The left side becomes ∫_N Vol_J(F^{-1}(p)) dp. The right side is ∫_y Jac_q(dF) |J·V(F)| dvol, which is at most Λ∫_y |J·V(F)| because the q-Jacobian is bounded by the q-dilation. The key linear algebra estimate is: for any m-plane P⊂R^n, sup over k-planes Q⊂P of |J·Q| equals (sum_{I⊃J}|I·P|^2)^{1/2}. To prove it, rotate P by an orthogonal map fixing the coordinate plane J so that P∩J^⊥ is a coordinate (m−k)-plane K. Writing P=Q_0⊕K, any Q⊂P has |J·Q|≤|J·Q_0|, and the only nonzero term on the right is I=J∪K, giving equality. Hence |J·V(F)(x)|≤sum_{I⊃J}|I·T_x y|. Integrating gives ∫_N Vol_J(F^{-1}(p)) dp ≤ Λ sum_{I⊃J}Vol_I(y). Since there are only finitely many J, choose one regular value p for which all these averaged inequalities hold simultaneously, losing only a binomial constant absorbed into C(n).

statement: [arXiv:dspace_mit_edu_e84b9cfc56e7 Proposition 8.1.1] Let E and F be 3-dimensional ellipsoids in Euclidean 4-space, with principal axes R_1 ≤ R_2 ≤ R_3 ≤ R_4 and S_1 ≤ S_2 ≤ S_3 ≤ S_4, respectively. There are positive universal constants c and C with the following properties. If there is a diffeomorphism f:E→F with 2-dilation at most 1, then the following lower bounds hold: (B1) R_2 R_3 ≥ c S_2 S_3; (B2) R_2^2 R_3 R_4 ≥ c S_2^2 S_3 S_4; (B3a) if R_2^2 ≥ S_2 S_3, then R_2 R_3 R_4 ≥ c S_2^{1/2} S_3^{3/2} S_4; and (B3b) if R_2^2 ≤ S_2 S_3, then R_3 R_4 ≥ c S_3 S_4. Conversely, if the corresponding inequalities hold with a sufficiently large constant in place of the small constant, Guth constructs a diffeomorphism E→F whose 2-dilation is bounded by a universal constant.
proof_sketch: The proof has three independent lower-bound mechanisms. For (B1), the 2-width of a 3-dimensional ellipsoid with axes R_1≤R_2≤R_3≤R_4 is comparable to R_2R_3: the upper bound comes from slicing by the long coordinate, and the lower bound from a rectangular region quasi-isometric to R_2×R_3×R_4. A 2-contracting diffeomorphism cannot decrease the target 2-width when pulled back, so R_2R_3≳S_2S_3. For (B2), pack F by about N≈S_3S_4/S_2^2 disjoint subsets each quasi-isometric to an S_2-cube. Each has 2-width ≳S_2^2, hence each inverse image in E has 2-width ≳S_2^2. The width-volume inequality inside E gives volume at least ≳S_2^4/R_2 for each inverse image. Since the inverse images are disjoint and Vol(E)≈R_2R_3R_4, one gets R_2R_3R_4≳N S_2^4/R_2, i.e. R_2^2R_3R_4≳S_2^2S_3S_4. For (B3), place in F a solid torus modeled on a family of circles of length scale S_2 parametrized over a central S_3×S_4 rectangle. Each circle has filling area at least ≳S_2S_3 in F. Pulling back by the diffeomorphism, the transverse 2-area to the circle family cannot decrease, because f is 2-contracting. Also, if a curve in E has length L<cR_2, it lies in a uniformly Euclidean ball and fills with area ≤CL^2. Thus if R_2^2≥S_2S_3, each pulled-back circle has length ≳(S_2S_3)^{1/2}, giving preimage solid-torus volume ≳S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4, and hence R_2R_3R_4≳that quantity. If R_2^2≤S_2S_3, the same filling-area obstruction forces each pulled-back circle to have length ≳R_2, so the preimage has volume ≳R_2S_3S_4; comparing with Vol(E)≈R_2R_3R_4 gives R_3R_4≳S_3S_4. The converse uses explicit non-linear constructions: snake-map/quasi-isometric foldings and simple linear rescalings, with separate cases according to whether R_2^2 is above or below S_2S_3.

statement: [arXiv:dspace_mit_edu_e84b9cfc56e7 Proposition 3.2.1] Let R be an n-dimensional rectangle with side lengths R_1≤...≤R_n, and let U be an open subset of R. For 0≤l≤k<n, the k-width of U satisfies W_k(U) ≤ C(n) (R_1...R_l)^{(n-k)/(n-l)} Vol(U)^{(k-l)/(n-l)}. Here W_k(U) is the infimum of W such that there is a generic piecewise-linear map π:U→R^{n-k} all of whose fibers have k-dimensional volume at most W. The case l=0 is the width-volume inequality W_k(U)≤C(n)Vol(U)^{k/n}, while the case l=k gives the trivial rectangle bound W_k(U)≤C(n)R_1...R_k.
proof_sketch: The endpoint l=0 is Theorem 3.1: for a compactly supported continuous function 0≤f≤1 on R^n, one builds a generic PL projection whose fibers have f-mass ≤C(∫f)^{k/n}; approximating the characteristic function of U gives W_k(U)≤CVol(U)^{k/n}. The endpoint l=k follows because U⊂R and projection to the last n−k coordinates has fibers contained in k-rectangles of volume R_1...R_k. For 0<l<k, approximate 1_U from above by a continuous f supported near U with 0≤f≤1 and ∫f≈Vol(U). Average f over the first l coordinates: g(y)=(R_1...R_l)^{-1}∫_{[0,R_1]×...×[0,R_l]} f(x,y) dx, where y lies in the (n−l)-rectangle R_{l+1}×...×R_n. Then 0≤g≤1 and ∫g≈Vol(U)/(R_1...R_l). Apply the width-volume inequality in dimension n−l to g with width dimension k−l, obtaining a generic PL map π_0 in the y-variables whose fibers have g-mass ≤C(Vol(U)/(R_1...R_l))^{(k-l)/(n-l)}. Lift it to U by π(x,y)=π_0(y). Each π-fiber is the product of an l-rectangle of volume R_1...R_l with a π_0-fiber, so its f-mass, hence the volume of U in that fiber, is at most (R_1...R_l) times the preceding bound. Rearranging gives the stated exponent: C(R_1...R_l)^{(n-k)/(n-l)}Vol(U)^{(k-l)/(n-l)}.

statement: [arXiv:dspace_mit_edu_e84b9cfc56e7 Theorem 4.1] Let U be a bounded open subset of R^n with smooth boundary. Then ∂U contains pairwise disjoint subsets S_i such that Vol(U) ≤ C(n) Σ_i W_{n-2}(S_i)^{n/(n-2)}, where W_{n-2} denotes (n−2)-width. In particular, in dimension n=4 this reads Vol(U) ≤ C Σ_i W_2(S_i)^2.
proof_sketch: Call a ball B(p,R) good if W_{n-2}(∂U∩B(p,R))≥cR^{n-2}. The first step is to show that for every p∈U some radius R gives a good ball. For each R define Φ_R:R^n→S^n by mapping B(p,R) to the complement of the south pole via the exponential map and collapsing the complement of B(p,R) to the south pole. On ∂U×(0,∞), put a metric g that on the slice ∂U×{R} is essentially (π/R)^2 times the original metric on ∂U∩B(p,R), very small outside that ball, and very large in the R-direction. Then Φ(x,R)=Φ_R(x) is contracting. For large A, after a harmless pinch near the poles, Φ gives a degree-one map from (∂U×[A^{-1},A],g) to the unit S^n, sending each boundary component to a point. Gromov's width lower bound for a nonzero-degree map to S^n gives W_{n-2}(∂U×[A^{-1},A],g)≥c. The slicing lemma says that for a metric on M×[0,1], W_{m-1}(M×[0,1])≤2 sup_t W_{m-1}(M,g_t). Applying it with M=∂U yields some R for which the slice ∂U×{R}, hence ∂U∩B(p,R) after rescaling by R, has (n−2)-width ≥cR^{n-2}. Thus every p∈U is covered by a good ball. The Vitali covering lemma selects pairwise disjoint good balls B(p_i,R_i) with Σ_i R_i^n ≥ c Vol(U). Setting S_i=∂U∩B(p_i,R_i), goodness gives W_{n-2}(S_i)^{n/(n-2)} ≥ cR_i^n. Summing over i gives the theorem.

statement: [arXiv:math/0609569 Proposition 2.2] Let R = [0,R1] × ... × [0,Rn] be an n-dimensional Euclidean rectangle with 0 < R1 ≤ ... ≤ Rn, and let U be an open subset of R. For 0 ≤ l ≤ k < n, let W_k(U) denote the k-width of U, i.e. the infimum, over all families of relative flat k-cycles sweeping out U, of the supremal k-mass of a cycle in the family. Then there is a constant C(n), depending only on n, such that W_k(U) < C(n) (R1...Rl)^((n−k)/(n−l)) Vol_n(U)^((k−l)/(n−l)). The empty product for l=0 is interpreted as 1.
proof_sketch: The endpoint l=0 is the width-volume inequality. After scaling to Vol(U)=1, translate the unit cubical lattice so that its k-skeleton S meets U in k-measure O_n(1), by averaging over translations. Construct a degree-one proper PL map Ψ of R^n which collapses most of space to S: good simplices map into S, while bad simplices lie in a small neighborhood of the dual (n−k−1)-skeleton. Applying Ψ to a generic family of parallel k-planes gives a sweepout; genericity ensures each plane meets only O_n(1) bad simplices, so each image cycle lies in S with bounded multiplicity except for O_n(1) additional k-mass. Restricting to U gives mass O_n(1), and rescaling gives W_k(U) ≤ C Vol(U)^{k/n}. The endpoint l=k follows from monotonicity W_k(U) ≤ W_k(R) and the standard sweepout of R by k-rectangles of volume R1...Rk. For intermediate l, define a function f on the (n−l)-rectangle [0,R_{l+1}]×...×[0,R_n] by f(y)=(R1...Rl)^{-1} Vol_l(U ∩ ([0,R1]×...×[0,Rl]×{y})). Then 0≤f≤1 and ∫f=Vol(U)/(R1...Rl). Apply the function version of the width-volume inequality in dimension n−l to get a family F of (k−l)-cycles with ∫_{F(p)} f ≤ C(∫f)^{(k−l)/(n−l)}. Taking products F(p)×[0,R1]×...×[0,Rl] gives k-cycles sweeping out the corresponding product region; restricting them to U yields k-mass at most (R1...Rl) C(Vol(U)/(R1...Rl))^{(k−l)/(n−l)}, which is exactly the displayed bound.

statement: [arXiv:math/0609569 Proposition 3.2] Let f be a piecewise smooth map between n-dimensional Riemannian manifolds. Suppose that its k-dilation satisfies Dil_k(f) ≤ λ for some 1 ≤ k < n. Then for every integer i with 1 ≤ i ≤ n−k, the (k+i)-dilation satisfies Dil_{k+i}(f) ≤ λ^((k+i)/k). In particular, if Dil_k(f) ≤ 1, then Dil_j(f) ≤ 1 for every j ≥ k.
proof_sketch: At each differentiability point, let 0 ≤ s1 ≤ ... ≤ sn be the singular values of df. By the exterior-power characterization of dilation, the pointwise k-Jacobian norm is s_{n−k+1}...s_n, and the pointwise (k+i)-Jacobian norm is s_{n−k−i+1}...s_n. Since s_{n−k+1} is the smallest of the largest k singular values, s_{n−k+1}^k ≤ s_{n−k+1}...s_n ≤ λ. The extra i factors in the (k+i)-fold product are all at most s_{n−k+1}, hence s_{n−k−i+1}...s_n ≤ s_{n−k+1}^i (s_{n−k+1}...s_n) ≤ λ^{i/k} λ = λ^{(k+i)/k}. Taking the supremum over all differentiability points gives the claimed dilation bound; the nonsmooth locus of a piecewise smooth map is irrelevant for the supremum over smooth pieces.

statement: [arXiv:math/0609569 Proposition 3.4] Let R and S be n-dimensional Euclidean rectangles with side lengths R1 ≤ ... ≤ Rn and S1 ≤ ... ≤ Sn, respectively, and put Q_i = S_i/R_i. Let U be a bounded open subset of R for which degree-one maps of pairs (U,∂U) → (S,∂S) are defined. Let D_k(U,S) be the infimum of the k-dilation over all such degree-one maps. For every 1 ≤ k < n and every integer l with 0 ≤ l ≤ k, there is a constant c(n)>0 depending only on n such that D_k(U,S) ≥ c(n) Q1...Ql (Q_{l+1}...Q_n)^((k−l)/(n−l)). The empty product Q1...Ql is interpreted as 1 when l=0.
proof_sketch: Introduce the packing width P_{k,N}(X): the supremal W such that X contains N disjoint open subsets each having k-width at least W. If f:U→S has degree 1 and k-dilation λ, and S_i are N disjoint subsets of S, then U_i=f^{-1}(S_i) are disjoint in U and, by excision/naturality of degree, f restricts to a nonzero-degree map (U_i,∂U_i)→(S_i,∂S_i). Monotonicity of k-width under maps with k-dilation λ gives W_k(U_i) ≥ W_k(S_i)/λ. Thus λ P_{k,N}(U) ≥ P_{k,N}(S). Since U⊂R, P_{k,N}(U) ≤ P_{k,N}(R), so λ ≥ P_{k,N}(S)/P_{k,N}(R) for every N. The needed estimates for P_{k,N} are obtained in Proposition 2.3: for a rectangle A with sides A1≤...≤An, P_{k,N}(A) is, up to C(n), the minimum over 0≤j≤k of A1...Aj (A_{j+1}...A_n)^((k−j)/(n−j)) N^{−(k−j)/(n−j)}. The upper bound uses Proposition 2.2 on one of N disjoint sets of volume at most Vol(A)/N; the lower bound comes from cutting A into a rectangular grid of N subrectangles of dimensions A1×...×Aj×L×...×L. Choosing N at the scale for which the l-th grid term is realized in S, e.g. N≈S_{l+1}...S_n/S_l^{n−l} for 1≤l≤k with endpoint modifications for l=0,k, gives P_{k,N}(S) ≥ c S1...Sl S_l^{k−l}, while P_{k,N}(R) is bounded above by the corresponding l-term. Algebra yields P_{k,N}(S)/P_{k,N}(R) ≥ c Q1...Ql(Q_{l+1}...Q_n)^((k−l)/(n−l)). Hence every degree-one f has at least this k-dilation. The paper also notes an additional elementary lower bound in the special case k=n−1: if f:R→S has (n−1)-dilation λ, then f|∂R is surjective, so λ Vol_{n−1}(∂R) ≥ Vol_{n−1}(∂S); since Vol(∂R)≤2n R2...Rn and Vol(∂S)≥2 S2...Sn, one gets λ ≥ c(n) Q2...Qn.

statement: [arXiv:math/0702066 Section 1: Almgren gluing homomorphism and the fundamental cohomology class a(k,n)] Let B^n be the closed unit n-ball and let Z(k,n) be the metric completion, in the area-distance/flat topology, of mod-2 relative Lipschitz k-cycles in (B^n,∂B^n). If X is a simplicial complex and F:X→Z(k,n) is a continuous family of k-cycles, then F canonically determines a gluing homomorphism G_F:H_i(X;Z2)→H_{k+i}(B^n,∂B^n;Z2) for every i. Concretely, after choosing a sufficiently fine triangulation of X and a sufficiently small parameter δ, one may choose for every i-face A of X a relative mod-2 Lipschitz (k+i)-chain C(A) such that whenever ∂A=Σ B_j, one has ∂C(A)=Σ C(B_j), and such that at vertices v the cycle C(v) is δ-close to F(v). This chain map induces G_F. The induced homomorphism is independent of all choices, is unchanged by refining the triangulation, and is homotopy invariant in F. In particular, applying this construction to cycles in the space Z(k,n) itself gives a homomorphism H_{n-k}(Z(k,n);Z2)→H_n(B^n,∂B^n;Z2)=Z2; by the universal coefficient theorem this homomorphism is represented by a canonical class a(k,n)∈H^{n-k}(Z(k,n);Z2). For every family F:X→Z(k,n) and every h∈H_{n-k}(X;Z2), the pairing ⟨F^*a(k,n),h⟩ is exactly G_F(h). Thus F detects a(k,n) precisely when its gluing homomorphism in degree n−k is nonzero, i.e. when the family sweeps out the ball mod 2. For the standard family of parallel k-planes R^k×{t}, t∈[-1,1]^{n-k}, restricted to B^n and with boundary parameters sent to the empty relative cycle, the pullback of a(k,n) is the fundamental cohomology class of ([-1,1]^{n-k},∂[-1,1]^{n-k}).
proof_sketch: Choose a fine triangulation of X. At each vertex v choose a Lipschitz relative k-cycle C(v) close to F(v). If v0,v1 span an edge, continuity of F and fineness of the triangulation make C(v0)−C(v1) small in area-distance, so choose a small (k+1)-chain filling it. Inductively, suppose C has been chosen on the boundary of an i-simplex Δ. The boundary Σ C(∂Δ) is a relative (k+i−1)-cycle of very small volume. For k+i−1<n, fill it by Federer-Fleming isoperimetry; for dimension n, small volume forces degree zero and hence fillability; above dimension n fillability is automatic in this relative chain complex. This gives a chain map from simplicial chains of X to relative Lipschitz chains in B^n, shifted by k, hence a homology homomorphism G_F. If two choices, two δ's, or a refinement are used, build a chain map on X×[0,1] interpolating between them; the same inductive filling argument gives a chain homotopy, so G_F is canonical. A homotopy of families is handled identically on X×[0,1]. If z and z′ are homologous (n−k)-cycles in Z(k,n), glue over a chain with boundary z−z′ to see the resulting n-dimensional relative homology classes in B^n agree; hence the assignment on H_{n-k}(Z(k,n)) is well-defined and yields a cohomology class a(k,n). For parallel planes, take C(Δ)=B^n∩(R^k×Δ); summing over top-dimensional Δ gives the fundamental class of (B^n,∂B^n).

statement: [arXiv:math/0702066 Propositions 9.1 and 9.2: minimax comparison under degree-one k-dilation maps] Let (M,g) and (N,h) be compact n-dimensional Riemannian manifolds, using absolute mod-2 k-cycle spaces when the manifolds are closed and relative mod-2 k-cycle spaces for manifolds with boundary. Let a(k,M) and a(k,N) denote their fundamental cohomology classes in degree n−k, defined by the gluing construction. For a cohomology class β in the relevant cycle space, define V_{(M,g)}(β)=inf_F sup_{C in F} Vol_k(C), where F ranges over all continuous families of k-cycles detecting β. Suppose Φ:(M,g)→(N,h), or a map of pairs Φ:(M,∂M)→(N,∂N), is piecewise C^1, has degree 1 mod 2, and has k-dilation at most Λ, meaning every k-dimensional submanifold has image k-volume at most Λ times its k-volume. If O is any natural cohomology operation with mod-2 coefficients, for example a cup power or an iterated Steenrod square, then Λ V_{(M,g)}(O a(k,M)) ≥ V_{(N,h)}(O a(k,N)). More generally, if U⊂M is an open set with piecewise smooth boundary and Φ:(U,∂U)→(N,∂N) is piecewise C^1, degree 1 mod 2, and has k-dilation at most Λ, then the same inequality holds: Λ V_{(M,g)}(O a(k,M)) ≥ V_{(N,h)}(O a(k,N)).
proof_sketch: The key identity is naturality of the fundamental cohomology class under degree-one maps: the induced map on cycle spaces Z_k(Φ) satisfies Z_k(Φ)^*a(k,N)=(deg Φ)a(k,M), hence equals a(k,M) mod 2 when deg Φ=1. This follows directly from the gluing definition: glue a family of cycles in M, push the glued (k+i)-chains forward by Φ, and the top-dimensional glued class is pushed forward by the degree-one map to the fundamental class of N. Natural cohomology operations commute with pullback, so Z_k(Φ)^*(O a(k,N))=O a(k,M). Now choose, for arbitrary δ>0, a family F in M detecting Oa(k,M) with maximal k-volume at most V_{(M,g)}(Oa(k,M))+δ. Pushing every cycle forward by Φ gives a family in N detecting Oa(k,N), and the k-dilation hypothesis bounds every pushed-forward k-volume by Λ times the original volume. Therefore V_{(N,h)}(Oa(k,N))≤Λ(V_{(M,g)}(Oa(k,M))+δ), and δ→0 gives the result. In the relative U⊂M version, first restrict cycles of the family in M to relative cycles in U; the restriction map sends a(k,U) back to a(k,M) because restricting a glued fundamental cycle of M to U gives the relative fundamental class of (U,∂U). Then push forward by Φ and apply the same dilation estimate.

statement: [arXiv:math/0702066 Theorems 1 and 2: minimax bounds for cup powers and Steenrod towers in the unit ball] Let Z(k,n) be the space of mod-2 relative k-cycles in the unit n-ball and let a(k,n)∈H^{n-k}(Z(k,n);Z2) be the fundamental cohomology class. Theorem 1 states that there are constants 0<c(n)<C(n) such that for every positive integer p, c(n) p^{(n-k)/n} ≤ V(a(k,n)^p) ≤ C(n) p^{(n-k)/n}. For Steenrod squares, write Sq_i: H^N(X;Z2)→H^{2N-i}(X;Z2) for the lower-index operation Sq_i(α)=Sq^{N-i}(α), and define iterates Sq_i^Q by repeated application. Theorem 2 states that for every ε>0 there is c(n,ε)>0 and there is C(n), independent of ε, such that for all nonnegative integers Q_0,...,Q_{n-k-1}, c(n,ε) Π_{i=0}^{n-k-1} (2−ε)^{((n-k-i)/(n-i)) Q_i} ≤ V(Sq_0^{Q_0} Sq_1^{Q_1} ... Sq_{n-k-1}^{Q_{n-k-1}} a(k,n)) ≤ C(n) Π_{i=0}^{n-k-1} 2^{((n-k-i)/(n-i)) Q_i}.
proof_sketch: Lower bound for cup powers: introduce V_+, where V_+(α)≥V means that in every family F:X→Z(k,n), F^*α vanishes on a neighborhood of the set of cycles of volume ≤V. Gromov's filling argument gives V_+(a(k,n))≥c(n): if all cycles have tiny filling volume, the complex of cycles can be filled over X×[0,1] to the zero family, so it cannot sweep out. For a^p, place p disjoint balls B_i of radius r≈p^{-1/n}. Let S_i be cycles whose restriction to B_i has k-volume ≤const·r^k. By scaling and V_+(a)>0, a vanishes near S_i. Lusternik-Schnirelmann theory says a^p vanishes near ∪S_i. Thus a family detecting a^p contains a cycle outside all S_i, with volume at least p·const·r^k≈p^{(n-k)/n}. A more general cup product theorem optimizes unequal radii and gives the same mechanism. Lower bound for Steenrod towers: replace LS theory by the paper's Vanishing Lemma. If π:S^i×X→X and π^*α vanishes on an open V⊂S^i×X, then Sq_i α vanishes on P[V]=∩_{θ∈S^i}(V(θ)∪V(−θ)). Iterating this over an i-pyramid of convex subsets of B^n converts smallness of cycle pieces in many convex sets into vanishing of Sq_i^Pα. The pyramids are built by repeated equal-volume bisection by hyperplanes whose normals form S^i-families with any n−i consecutive normals orthogonal. The hard geometric estimate controls the pyramid thickness: along the worst bisection tree, estimate Σ Rad(C_I)^{-1} using N_β(C)=sup_a β^a Vol(C)^{-1/a}π_{n-a}(C)^{1/a}, where π_q is average q-dimensional projection volume. If N_β is attained at a, then the approximating rectangle has R_{a+1}≫R_a, so after n−i orthogonal cuts the relevant projected volumes grow by at most (2+ε)^a. Blocking the tree gives thickness ≥c(n,ε)(2−ε)^{((n-i-k)/(n-i))P}. Iterating over i and using V_+(a)>0 proves the stated lower bound. Upper bounds: first build detecting families of 0-cycles. For n−k=1 use roots of real polynomials; for n−k≥2 use symmetric products SP^p S^{n-k} and Nakaoka's cohomology computation. Suspend k times to obtain families of unions of parallel k-planes detecting the desired classes. For Steenrod towers, use translation/suspension and addition on symmetric products to model the Steenrod operations. Then apply bend-and-cancel: after a generic rotation, a PL skeleton-squeezing map sends most of each plane into a lattice k-skeleton, with only O(1) bad pieces per plane; mod-2 overlap cancellation leaves volume bounded by skeleton volume plus exceptional pieces. For Steenrod towers use a multiscale sequence of skeleton-squeezing maps, producing exactly the product of powers in the upper bound.

statement: [arXiv:0710.0403 Theorem 3] Let R=[0,R_1]×...×[0,R_n] be an n-dimensional rectangle with 0<R_1≤...≤R_n. For a relative integral k-cycle z in (R,∂R), let FV_R(z) be the infimum of the masses of relative integral (k+1)-chains y in R with ∂y=z, and define I_R^k(V)=sup{FV_R(z): z is a relative integral k-cycle in R with mass |z|≤V}. For each n there are constants c(n)>0 and C(n)<∞ such that, for every 1≤k≤n−1, the following hold. If V≤c(n)R_1⋯R_k and V is written in the form V=c(n)R_1⋯R_j ρ^{k−j} for some j∈{0,...,k−1}, with empty product R_1⋯R_0=1, where 0<ρ≤R_1 when j=0 and R_j≤ρ≤R_{j+1} when j≥1, then I_R^k(V)≤C(n)R_1⋯R_j ρ^{k−j+1}. Without any smallness assumption on V, I_R^k(V)≤C(n)R_{k+1}V. Consequently, with possibly different dimensional constants c,C, if z is a relative p-cycle in R, 1≤j≤p≤n−1, and |z|≤c(n)R_1⋯R_j R_j^{p−j}, then z bounds a relative (p+1)-chain in R of mass at most C(n)R_j|z|.
proof_sketch: The proof is an induction on k using the Federer-Fleming deformation theorem adapted to a rectangular lattice. The deformation lemma says: for a relative k-cycle z and a rectangular lattice in R with all cell side lengths ∼L≤R_1 and ∂R in the (n−1)-skeleton, z is homologous to a relative k-cycle z′ in the k-skeleton, with |z′|≤C|z| and homology mass ≤CL|z|. It is proved by pushing z successively from the p-skeleton to the (p−1)-skeleton: in each p-face choose a random center and radially project to the boundary; average stretching is O_n(1), and the swept homology costs O_n(L) times the mass. Base k=0: a weighted sum of points is filled by joining each point to the face x_1=0, costing ≤R_1|z|. Inductive step, small-volume case V≤cR_1^k: take L≈V^{1/k}≤R_1. After deformation, |z′|< one k-cell mass, so integrality forces z′=0; the deformation homology fills z with mass O(LV)=O(V^{(k+1)/k}), which is the j=0 profile. Large case V≥cR_1^k: take L=R_1 and choose the lattice so each lattice vertex has x_1-coordinate 0 or R_1. Then every interior k-face contains the whole [0,R_1] factor, so the deformed cycle has product form z′=[0,R_1]×z_1 in the (n−1)-rectangle [0,R_2]×...×[0,R_n], with |z_1|≤CV/R_1. Apply the induction hypothesis to z_1. If V=cR_1⋯R_jρ^{k−j}, then z_1 has the corresponding profile with index j−1 and the same ρ, giving a filling C_1 of mass ≤CR_2⋯R_jρ^{k−j+1}; multiplying by [0,R_1] gives ≤CR_1⋯R_jρ^{k−j+1}. The deformation homology costs ≤CR_1V, which is ≤CR_1⋯R_jρ^{k−j+1} since ρ≥R_1 in this case. For the global bound, induction gives z_1 a filling of mass ≤CR_{k+1}V/R_1; multiplying by R_1 and adding the deformation cost CR_1V≤CR_{k+1}V proves I_R^k(V)≤CR_{k+1}V.

statement: [arXiv:0710.0403 Lemma 4.1] Let S=[0,S_1]×...×[0,S_n] be an n-dimensional rectangle with 0<S_1≤...≤S_n. A complex of cycles in S parametrized by a finite oriented polyhedral complex X means a chain map C from the cellular chain complex of X to the complex I_rel(S) of relative integral Lipschitz chains in (S,∂S), so that each p-face F^p is assigned a relative p-chain C(F^p) and boundaries are respected. There is a constant c(n)>0 such that if C_0 is a complex of cycles in S parametrized by X, C_0(v)=0 for every vertex v of X, and |C_0(F^p)|≤c(n)S_1⋯S_p for every p-face F^p of X with 1≤p≤n, then C_0 is null-homotopic: there exists a complex of cycles on X×[0,1] restricting to C_0 on X×{0} and to the zero complex on X×{1}. The same proof applies to complexes parametrized by a pair such as (R,∂R), giving a homotopy parametrized by (R×[0,1],∂R×[0,1]).
proof_sketch: Construct the homotopy C on X×[0,1] skeleton by skeleton. Set the target endpoint to the zero complex. Since C_0(v)=0, define C(v×[0,1])=0 for each vertex, starting the induction. Assume C has been defined on the (p−1)-skeleton of X×[0,1] with the intended mass bounds. For a p-face E of X×[0,1], the already-defined C(∂E) is a relative (p−1)-cycle in S. After taking X simplicial or absorbing the bounded number of facets of each face into constants, its mass is ≤c′(n)S_1⋯S_{p−1}. Choose the initial c(n) small enough so Theorem 3 applies in the easy small-profile range; C(∂E) then bounds a relative p-chain of mass ≤c(n)S_1⋯S_p. Define C(E) to be such a filling. This inductively extends C through the n-skeleton while preserving smallness. For an (n+1)-face, the boundary is a relative n-cycle of mass <S_1⋯S_n if c(n) is small. A nonzero class in H_n(S,∂S;Z) is an integer multiple of the fundamental class and has mass at least Vol(S)=S_1⋯S_n, so this n-cycle is exact; fill it arbitrarily. For all higher-dimensional faces there is no obstruction because H_q(S,∂S;Z)=0 for q≥n+1. Thus the homotopy to zero is completed.

statement: [arXiv:0710.0403 Estimate 1] Let R=[0,R_1]×...×[0,R_n] and S=[0,S_1]×...×[0,S_n] be n-dimensional rectangles with 0<R_1≤...≤R_n and 0<S_1≤...≤S_n, and put Q_i=S_i/R_i. For each n there is a constant c(n)>0 such that the following holds. Suppose U is an open subset of R and Φ:(U,∂U)→(S,∂S) is a smooth map of pairs of positive degree D. Then for every choice of integers j,k,l satisfying 0≤j<k≤l≤n, the k-dilation of Φ obeys Dil_k(Φ)≥c(n) Q_1⋯Q_j (Q_{j+1}⋯Q_l)^{(k−j)/(l−j)}, with the empty product interpreted as 1. Equivalently, after changing the dimensional constant, if Φ is k-contracting and has nonzero degree, then for all 0≤j<k≤l≤n one has (R_1⋯R_j)^{(l−j)/(k−j)} R_{j+1}⋯R_l ≥ c(n)(S_1⋯S_j)^{(l−j)/(k−j)} S_{j+1}⋯S_l.
proof_sketch: By scaling, prove the equivalent k-contracting inequalities. For j=0, decompose R into blocks R_1×...×R_k×ε×...×ε. The associated complex C_Φ sends p<k faces to zero, sends k-faces to chains of mass ≤R_1⋯R_k, and sends p>k faces to chains of mass ≤R_1⋯R_k ε^{p−k}, using the linear-algebra fact that k-contraction implies p-contraction for p≥k. If R_1⋯R_k<cS_1⋯S_k and ε is small, Lemma 4.1 makes C_Φ null-homotopic, contradicting nonzero degree. Applying this to l-dilation and using Dil_l^{k/l}≤Dil_k gives all j=0 cases. For 0<j<k<l, Section 5 reduces to the case L≤R_{j+1}, where L is defined by R_1⋯R_j L^{k−j}=δ(n)S_1⋯S_j S_j^{k−j}: for fixed l the desired inequality can be written with right side independent of j, so choose j minimizing the left side; Proposition 4.1 handles j=k−1, and comparison with j+1 gives L≤R_{j+1} otherwise. Subdivide R into blocks of dimensions R_1×...×R_j×L×...×L, and let B be the complex of interior faces. Let C_0(F)=Φ(F∩U). Build a tightened complex C_1 agreeing with C_0 on the k-skeleton. Inductively, if C_1 is defined through dimension p, then for each (p+1)-face the boundary C_1(∂F) has mass ≤O(δ S_1⋯S_j S_j^{p−j}); Theorem 3 fills it by a (p+1)-chain of mass ≤O(δ S_1⋯S_j S_j^{p+1−j}). The key point is deg C_1=deg C_0=D. This is proved by gradual tightening on dyadic refinements B_s with mesh 2^{−s}L. Let Γ_s be the tightened complex on B_s. The glued Γ_{s+1} and Γ_s agree on the k-skeleton and have all higher faces small, so Lemma 6.1, a two-complex version of Lemma 4.1, homotopes them. For large s, the actual image complex β_s(F)=Φ(F∩U) has higher-dimensional face masses tiny and is homotopic to Γ_s; β_s has degree D. Hence C_1 has degree D. Now glue C_1 over the coarser subdivision with blocks R_1×...×R_l×L×...×L. A p-face in the coarse complex is a union of N=(R_{j+1}/L)⋯(R_l/L) fine p-faces, so |C_1^+(F^p)|≤Cδ N S_1⋯S_j S_j^{p−j}=C(δ)[(R_1⋯R_j)/(S_1⋯S_j)]^{(l−k)/(k−j)} R_1⋯R_l S_j^{p−l}. Since C_1^+ has nonzero degree, Lemma 4.1 implies some p-face has mass ≥cS_1⋯S_p. Comparing and using S_{l+1},...,S_p≥S_j gives [(R_1⋯R_j)/(S_1⋯S_j)]^{(l−k)/(k−j)}R_1⋯R_l≥cS_1⋯S_l, equivalent to the stated estimate.

statement: Known tool from Guth: for an n-rectangle R with side lengths R1 <= ... <= Rn, the relative k-cycle filling profile satisfies: if V <= c R1...Rk and V = c R1...Rj rho^(k-j) with Rj <= rho <= R_{j+1}, then I_R^k(V) <= C R1...Rj rho^(k-j+1); in any case I_R^k(V) <= C R_{k+1} V.
Known tool from Guth estimates for n=4,k=2,D=1,Dil_2<=1: R1 R2 >= c S1 S2, R1 R2 R3 >= c S1 S2 S3, Vol(R) >= c Vol(S), R1^2 R2 R3 >= c S1^2 S2 S3, and R1^2 Vol(R) >= c S1^3 S2 S3 S4.

approach: The naive pointwise source lemma for arbitrary relative 2-cycles is likely false. In normalized variables S1=S2=1, S3=q, S4=L, one can choose a very thin R1 and large R2=R3 so that a relative cylinder [0,R1] times a circle in the x2x3-plane has filling volume about q but area o(sqrt(q)), while still satisfying the standard monomial estimates and the negations of the desired conclusions for suitable q,L.

value: Need a family-level obstruction for the central fibers z_y = (pi_34 o f)^{-1}(y), probably via Almgren complexes, tightening, Sq_1/minimax, or a solid-torus/linking construction. Isolated filling-volume lower bounds are insufficient.
Prove that the 2-parameter family of central fibers has large average area, not merely that each fiber has large filling volume.

note: Use Guth's standard monomial estimates only as background; they do not see the sqrt(S3/S2) factor. The local arbitrary-cycle filling lemma is likely false, so future work should focus on the fact that the cycles are a 2-parameter family of degree-one fibers, not isolated cycles.

statement: For every good regular y in the interior of the base, z_y is a relative integral 2-cycle in (R,partial R), and f_# z_y equals [[P_y]] as an integral current in S; equivalently the relative class is [P_y] in H_2(P_y,partial P_y).
proof_sketch: The boundary of the fiber lies in partial R. Naturality of cap product with the local orientation class of y gives the relative class [P_y], and the constancy theorem upgrades this to current equality.

statement: Every relative filling Y of z_y in R pushes forward to a relative filling of P_y in S, and if Dil_2(f) <= 1 then FillVol_R(z_y) >= FillVol_S(P_y).
proof_sketch: The map sends boundary-supported errors into partial S and f_# z_y = P_y. The singular-value inequality Dil_2 <= 1 implies Dil_3 <= 1, so pushforward does not increase 3-mass.

statement: The correct target filling formula is FillVol_S(P_y) = S1 S2 dist((y3,y4), partial([0,S3]x[0,S4])); in particular it is at least (1/3) S1 S2 S3 on the doubly central base.
proof_sketch: A relative calibration rho(x3,x4) dx1 wedge dx2 gives the lower bound, and the product of P_y with a shortest base segment to the boundary gives the matching upper bound.

statement: The coarea estimate integral over [0,S3]x[0,S4] of Area(z_y) dy <= Vol(R) holds.
proof_sketch: Coarea gives the integral as the integral of Jac_2(pi o f), and Jac_2(pi o f) <= Dil_2(f) <= 1.

statement: If Area(z_y) >= a S1 sqrt(S2 S3) for a.e. y in the y3-central base, then Vol(R) >= (a/3) S1 S2^(1/2) S3^(3/2) S4; if it holds on a theta fraction, the constant is theta a/3.
proof_sketch: Integrate the assumed fiber-area lower bound over the relevant subset of the base and apply the coarea estimate.

reason: It is false under the stated relative boundary: if y4 = epsilon, the prism [0,S1]x[0,S2]x{y3}x[0,epsilon] fills P_y with mass S1 S2 epsilon.

statement: The suspected pointwise counterexample is valid: in the normalized case S1=S2=1, S3=q, S4=L, the described rectangle R contains relative 2-cycles with FillVol ~ q but area o(sqrt(q)).
proof_sketch: Compute the side products directly. The cycle [0,R1] times a circle of radius rho ~ sqrt(q/R1) has area ~ sqrt(R1 q) and filling volume ~ R1 rho^2 ~ q; the lower filling bound follows by slicing any filling by x1.

statement: A family lemma using only Almgren degree, central filling lower bounds, and the standard monomial estimates is false.
proof_sketch: For S=(1,1,q,q) and R=(kappa,kappa^-4,q kappa^2,q/kappa), the coordinate-sheet family z_{u,v}=[0,R1]x[0,R2]x{uR3/q}x{vR4/q} is a degree-one sweepout with central FillVol >= c q but area kappa^-3 << sqrt(q) for q large.

statement: For actual fibers of a 2-dilation map, if sigma1 >= sigma2 are the singular values of d(pi_34 o f) normal to a regular fiber, then J2((f1,f2)|fiber) <= sigma1^-2.
proof_sketch: For a tangent vector v to the fiber and a normal vector n realizing sigma1, the component d(f1,f2)(v) wedge d(f3,f4)(n) is bounded by Dil_2(f) <= 1, forcing |d(f1,f2)(v)| <= sigma1^-1.

statement: The original theorem follows from the base-stretch dispersion estimate int sigma1^2 dmu >= c sqrt(S3/S2) mu, where dmu=(sigma2/sigma1) dx on the central preimage.
proof_sketch: The mixed-Jacobian lemma and fiber degree give mu >= c S1 S2 S3 S4. Coarea gives int area(z_y) dy = int sigma1^2 dmu, so the dispersion estimate implies Vol(R) >= c S1 S2^(1/2) S3^(3/2) S4.

reason: False: thin cylinders in the proposed rectangle have filling volume ~ S1 S2 S3 but area far below S1 sqrt(S2 S3).

approach: Pure Almgren degree-one family plus fill lower
reason: False: coordinate-sheet sweepouts can have degree one and large central filling volume while their areas are much smaller than the desired lower bound.
It gives the correct structural replacement for arbitrary-family arguments, but still needs a global base-stretch dispersion estimate using the side hypotheses R1 small and R3 R4 small.

statement: Literature check confirms that the published tightening-complex estimate gives the known monomial lower bounds for degree maps U subset R to S, while the minimax comparison with cohomology operations is a plausible but nontrivial route to stronger rectangle estimates.
proof_sketch: Guth's area-expanding paper states Estimate 1 for k-dilation maps of pairs and proves it by tightening complexes using the rectangular isoperimetric profile. Guth's minimax appendix states the cycle-space comparison proposition and then a rectangle minimax conjecture that would imply the thesis inequalities.

value: Prove the base-stretch dispersion estimate for actual fibers: average of σ1^2 with respect to dμ=(σ2/σ1)dx over the central preimage must be at least c sqrt(S3/S2).

Alternatively identify a concrete Sq_1, cup-Steenrod, or solid-torus minimax class whose target minimax volume is about S1 sqrt(S2 S3) and whose source minimax volume is small under the negation of both desired conclusions.
Establish a family-level obstruction for actual central fibers, using either mixed-Jacobian dispersion or a Steenrod-square/solid-torus minimax argument.

note: The standard Guth monomial estimates are confirmed but insufficient for the sqrt(S3/S2) gain. The key distinction is actual fibers of a 2-contracting degree-one map, encoded either by the mixed-Jacobian measure dμ or by a stronger cohomology operation such as Sq_1. Avoid pointwise filling lemmas and arbitrary two-parameter sweepout lemmas, both are already false.

statement: Central-fiber, filling-volume, coarea, and mixed-Jacobian reduction for degree-one 2-contracting maps between 4-rectangles.
For a 4-rectangle S=[0,S1]x[0,S2]x[0,S3]x[0,S4], the ordinary mod-2 relative 2-cycle minimax satisfies V_S(Sq_1 a_S) <= 2 S1 S2.
proof_sketch: Use the two-point mod-2 0-cycle space X=Sym^2(S^2)/Delta in the base B=[0,S3]x[0,S4]. This space detects Sq^1 of the fundamental 0-cycle class. Crossing each 0-cycle with A=[0,S1]x[0,S2] gives relative 2-cycles of area at most 2S1S2 and detects Sq_1 a_S.

statement: The coordinate-sheet counterfamily from t2 has a two-sheet enlargement that detects Sq_1 a, with maximal area 2 R1 R2.
proof_sketch: Replace the one-point base sweepout by the two-point mod-2 0-cycle family in the R3xR4 base and cross with [0,R1]x[0,R2]. The same Bockstein/Sq^1 calculation shows detection of Sq_1 a_R.

statement: A target lower bound V_S(Sq_1 a_S) >= c S1 sqrt(S2 S3) is false for S3/S2 large.
proof_sketch: The explicit two-sheet family gives V_S(Sq_1 a_S) <= 2S1S2, which is much smaller than S1 sqrt(S2S3) when S3 >> S2.

reason: It is defeated by the two-vertical-sheet family, which detects Sq_1 a with area only 2S1S2.

approach: Use the distinction that coordinate-sheet families detect a but not Sq_1 a
reason: The original one-sheet two-parameter family detects only a, but its natural two-point enlargement detects Sq_1 a.

approach: Solid-torus cycles [0,S1] x gamma as a minimax representative
reason: If their only certified invariant is an ordinary cycle-space class such as Sq_1 a, the two-sheet family gives a much smaller representative; additionally, 2-dilation does not control inverse images of arbitrary chosen target surfaces.

statement: At every regular point of a fiber z_y=F^{-1}(y), J_2(G|_{Tz_y}) <= min(1, sigma_1^{-2}).
proof_sketch: The tangent-tangent 2-dilation bound gives J_2(G|_{Tz_y}) <= 1. Pairing a tangent vector with a normal vector realizing sigma_1 gives |dG(v)| sigma_1 <= 1, hence J_2(G|_{Tz_y}) <= sigma_1^{-2}.

statement: The measure dnu = min(1, sigma_1^{-2}) J_2F dx satisfies f_#nu >= Lebesgue measure on [0,S1]x[0,S2]xB0, and int_A J_2F dx = int_A max(1,sigma_1^2) dnu.
proof_sketch: On each regular fiber, the oriented area formula and degree-one property of G dominate Lebesgue measure on the S1xS2 fiber. Coarea in F then gives the pushforward domination. The energy identity follows by splitting into sigma_1 <= 1 and sigma_1 >= 1.

statement: A distributional estimate of the form nu0({sigma_1 <= L}) <= C(epsilon + L^2 sqrt(S2/S3)) nu0(A) for every degree-carrying submeasure nu0 would imply the desired mixed-exponent volume lower bound.
proof_sketch: Choose L^2 comparable to sqrt(S3/S2). If epsilon and the constant are small enough, at least half of nu0 lies where sigma_1^2 >= c sqrt(S3/S2), so int J_2F >= c sqrt(S3/S2) S1 S2 S3 S4.

statement: For central regular p in [S1/3,2S1/3]x[S2/3,2S2/3], the dual fiber Sigma_p=G^{-1}(p) satisfies FillVol_R(Sigma_p) >= c S1 S3 S4.
proof_sketch: Cap-product naturality gives F_# Sigma_p as the fundamental relative class of the S3xS4 base rectangle at p. Pushing forward fillings and using Dil_3(f)<=1 reduces to the exact target filling volume S3S4 dist(p,partial([0,S1]x[0,S2])).

approach: Use total mu as the degree-carrying measure
reason: Total mu can count low-stretch zero-degree or canceling sheets; the corrected measure nu or a selected submeasure nu0 is the stable degree-carrying object.
The estimate int J_2F controls only total fiber area and allows long fibers; it does not force a lower bound on the distribution of sigma_1 without the coupling to G.
The dual fibers have a strong target filling lower bound, but a matching upper-filling or width estimate for the actual fibers is still missing.

statement: For actual fibers of F=(f3,f4), the measure dnu = min(1,sigma_1^{-2}) J_2F dx is degree-carrying: f_#nu dominates Lebesgue measure on P x B0, where P=[0,S1]x[0,S2] and B0 is a central subrectangle of [0,S3]x[0,S4].
proof_sketch: On each regular F-fiber, G=(f1,f2) has relative degree one onto P. The area formula and the pointwise estimate J_2(G restricted to the fiber) <= min(1,sigma_1^{-2}) imply the pushforward domination after coarea in F.

statement: The energy identity integral_A J_2F dx = integral_A max(1,sigma_1^2) dnu holds, and a low-stretch exclusion for a degree-carrying submeasure nu0 would imply the original mixed volume lower bound.
proof_sketch: On {sigma_1<=1}, dnu=J_2F dx; on {sigma_1>=1}, dnu=(sigma_2/sigma_1) dx, so J_2F dx=sigma_1^2 dnu. If at least a fixed fraction of nu0 has sigma_1^2 >= c sqrt(S3/S2), then the coarea integral gives Vol(R) >= c S1 S2^{1/2} S3^{3/2} S4.

statement: Ordinary Sq_1 a minimax cannot be the missing obstruction, since V_S(Sq_1 a_S) <= 2 S1 S2 for S=[0,S1]x[0,S2]x[0,S3]x[0,S4].
proof_sketch: Take the two-point mod-2 zero-cycle family in the base [0,S3]x[0,S4] and cross it with [0,S1]x[0,S2]. It detects Sq_1 a but every cycle has area at most 2 S1 S2.

approach: Ordinary Steenrod-square minimax on the relative 2-cycle space
reason: The two-sheet coordinate family detects Sq_1 a with area only 2 S1 S2, far below S1 sqrt(S2 S3) when S3 is large.

approach: Using total mu=(sigma_2/sigma_1) dx instead of a selected degree-carrying submeasure
reason: Total mu can include low-stretch null or canceling sheets and need not reflect the degree-one sheet covering P x B0.

approach: Using only dual central-fiber filling lower bounds
reason: The dual fibers have large filling volume in R, but no known upper filling or width bound for those actual fibers is strong enough by itself.

value: Prove the t3 low-stretch exclusion for the degree-carrying submeasure nu0.
Find or reconstruct Guth's original solid-torus or tightening proof for the four-dimensional rectangle mixed exponent.
Identify a stronger invariant that remembers actual fiber structure, fillings, or linking, not just ordinary mod-2 cycle-space cohomology.
Establish the base-stretch dispersion estimate for actual fibers of F=(f3,f4), or recover an equivalent solid-torus proof from the original area-contracting rectangle thesis.

note: Do not spend more effort on ordinary Sq_1 or arbitrary cycle-family minimax; t4 shows those invariants are blind to cheap multi-sheet coordinate families. The promising objects are actual fibers of F=(f3,f4), the measure nu0 from t3, and possibly the original solid-torus or tightening argument hidden in Guth's thesis.

statement: Conditional mixed-Jacobian reduction: a low-stretch exclusion for the degree-carrying measure implies the original rectangle theorem.
Directional coarea gives a central regular fiber z_y with all coordinate directional 2-volumes bounded by C Vol(R)/(S3 S4), and hence Area(z_y) <= C Vol(R)/(S3 S4).
proof_sketch: For each coordinate pair I, integrate the I-directional area of F^{-1}(y) over B0; the result is the integral of the complementary 2-minor of dF, which is bounded by Dil_2(f) <= 1.

statement: Combining the central filling lower bound with the relative rectangle filling profile proves the desired volume lower bound in the small-area branch Area(z_y) <= R1^2.
proof_sketch: Fill_R(z_y) >= c S1 S2 S3 and Fill_R(z_y) <= C Area(z_y)^{3/2}; using Area(z_y) <= C Vol(R)/(S3 S4) gives Vol(R) >= c(S1 S2 S3)^{2/3}S3S4, which is at least the desired mixed-exponent quantity.

statement: In the cylinder branch R1^2 <= Area(z_y) <= R1R2, the same argument gives only Vol(R) >= c S1 S2^{1/2} S3^{3/2} S4 sqrt(R1/S1).
proof_sketch: The sharp filling upper bound is Fill_R(z_y) <= C Area(z_y)^2/R1. Substituting the central filling lower bound and the coarea upper bound for area yields the stated weaker inequality.

statement: The directional-coarea route proves the theorem outside the hard regime R1 << epsilon^2 S1, R3 >> epsilon^{-1}sqrt(S2S3), and lambda=sqrt(S3/S2) >> epsilon^{-5}.
proof_sketch: The cylinder-branch lower bound contradicts Vol(R) <= epsilon T when R1 >= C epsilon^2 S1; the standard monomial estimate R1^2 Vol(R) >= c S1^3 S2 S3 S4 rules out R1 << epsilon^2 S1 unless lambda is very large. The directional filling bound with coefficient R3 handles R3 <= c epsilon^{-1}sqrt(S2S3).

statement: There is a sharp cylinder obstruction to the directional route: for S=(1,1,q,L) and R=(delta^{-1/2}q^{-1/4}, delta^{1/2}q^{3/4}, delta^{1/2}q^{3/4}, delta^{1/2}q^{1/4}L), a cylinder [0,R1] x S^1_rho has Fill ~ q but area ~ q^{3/8}.
proof_sketch: Taking rho ~ sqrt(q/R1), the cylinder has area R1 rho and filling volume R1 rho^2=q. The rectangle satisfies R3R4=delta S3S4 and Vol(R)=delta S1 S2^{1/2}S3^{3/2}S4, while standard monomial estimates are saturated.

statement: The naive measure bound for the low-stretch set E_L={sigma1<=L} gives only nu0(E_L)/nu0(A) <= C epsilon L^2 sqrt(S3/S2), which is far weaker than the desired C(epsilon+L^2 sqrt(S2/S3)).
proof_sketch: On E_L, dnu <= L^2 dx, so nu0(E_L) <= L^2 Vol(R); dividing by |P||B0| and using the contradiction volume hypothesis gives the wrong dependence.

reason: It fails in the sharp cylinder branch, where Fill ~ Area^2/R1 and the resulting lower bound loses a factor sqrt(R1/S1).
It gives Vol(R) >= c T sqrt(S2S3)/R3, which only helps when R3 is not much larger than sqrt(S2S3).

approach: Measure-only proof of the t3 low-stretch exclusion
reason: The pushforward identity f_#nu0=Lebesgue and pointwise bounds do not give current coherence; the selected measure can switch sheets, so slicing and filling lower bounds cannot be applied to E_L.

approach: Dual-fiber slicing of the low-stretch set
reason: The full dual fiber Sigma_p is a relative cycle with a filling lower bound, but E_L cap Sigma_p is generally not a relative cycle and has uncontrolled boundary.

statement: Central-fiber, filling-volume, coarea, and mixed-Jacobian reduction for degree-one 2-contracting maps between 4-rectangles.
proof_excerpt: We slice the degree‐one, 2‐contracting map f:R→S by projecting to the last two coordinates, producing 2‐cycles z_y in R whose push‐forwards fill the horizontal slices P_y⊂S; coarea then gives the area‐integral bound ∫_B Area(z_y)≤Vol(R), while isoperimetric (filling‐volume) estimates yield FillVol_R(z_y)≥S₁S₂·dist(y,∂B).  Restricting to the central box B₀, one defines the measure μ via the ratio σ₂/σ₁ of the singular values of dF and uses the key mixed‐Jacobian lemma—bounding the mixed wedge component of df to show J₂(G|_{Tz_y})≤σ₁⁻²—to prove μ(F⁻¹(B₀))≥(1/9)S₁S₂S₃S₄; together with an assumed L²‐lower bound on σ₁ over F⁻¹(B₀) this delivers the desired volume inequality R₁R₂R₃R₄≳S₁S₂^½S₃^3/2S₄.
problem_solved:
After the routine fixes above, the proof establishes the stated fiber-filling and coarea reduction: for oriented Euclidean \(4\)-rectangles \(R,S\), a finite piecewise smooth Lipschitz degree-one map \(f:(R,\partial R)\to(S,\partial S)\) with \(\operatorname{Dil}_2(f)\le1\), and \(F=\pi\circ f\), the a.e. slices \(z_y=\langle\llbracket R\rrbracket,F,y\rangle\) are relative integral \(2\)-cycles with \(f_\#z_y=\llbracket Q\times\{y\}\rrbracket\); they satisfy the filling lower bound and the coarea area bound; the measure \(\mu=(\sigma_2/\sigma_1)\,dx\) satisfies the claimed lower bound on \(F^{-1}(B_0)\) and the claimed identity for \(\int_{B_0}\operatorname{Area}(z_y)\,dy\); and any additional dispersion estimate of the stated form implies
\[
R_1R_2R_3R_4\ge \frac{c_d}{9}S_1S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4.
\]
It does not prove the dispersion estimate, the alternative \(R_3R_4>kS_3S_4\), or the original dichotomy.

statement: Conditional mixed-Jacobian reduction: a low-stretch exclusion for the degree-carrying measure implies the original rectangle theorem.
proof_excerpt: We construct a weighted Borel measure ν on the preimage A by dν(x)=m(σ₁(x))J₂F(x)dx, then use a smooth triangulation, Sard’s theorem and the coarea–area formulas on the level‐set surfaces M_y=F⁻¹(y), combined with a Brouwer‐degree count on each slice, to show f₍#₎ν dominates Lebesgue measure on Q.  A “moment” identity max(1,σ₁²) dν=J₂F dx lets us extract a submeasure ν₀ with exact mass |Q|, and a dispersion estimate on small σ₁ forces a lower bound on ∫_A J₂F ≤Vol(R), yielding the claimed volume bound.

statement:
Suppose $(M, g)$ and $(N,h)$ are piecewise smooth Riemannian manifolds.   If $f: M \rightarrow N$ is piecewise smooth,  we define the $k$-dilation of $f$ as the infimal $\Lambda$ so that for every $k$-dimensional submanifold $\Sigma \subset M$, 

$$ \Vol_k( f(\Sigma) ) \le \Lambda \Vol_k (\Sigma). $$

\noindent We write the $k$-dilation of $f$ as $\Dil_k(f)$.   Equivalently,  $\Dil_k(f) = \sup_{x \in M} \| \Lambda^k df \|$.

Prove that there is a constant $k > 0$ so that the following holds.

Suppose that $R \subset \RR^4$ is a 4-dimensional rectangle with side lengths $R_1 \le R_2 \le R_3 \le R_4$ and $S$ is a 4-dimensional rectangle with side lengths $S_1 \le S_2 \le S_3 \le S_4$.   Suppose that $f: (R, \partial R) \rightarrow (S, \partial S)$ is a piecewise smooth map with degree 1 and with $\Dil_2(f) \le 1$.   Suppose that $R_1 \le k S_1$.   Then either

$$ R_3 R_4 > k S_3 S_4,  or $$

$$ R_1 R_2 R_3 R_4 > k S_1 S_2^{1/2} S_3^{3/2} S_4. $$
